FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline 2016-2018, ages 9-12 years, n=8267, 21 U.S. recruitment sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; multivariable linear regression examining associations between screen time modalities (baseline and year 1) and MIND diet nutrition scores (year 1)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Academic Pediatrics, 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured separately by modality: television, videos (YouTube), video games, texting, video chat, social media. All modalities are phone/SM-specific isolates, not bundled. Outcome is MIND diet nutrition score (14-item FFQ measuring adherence to protective and neurodegenerative food groups), an individual-trait health/behavioral measure

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative inferential analysis comparing screen time against nutrition score; regression coefficients with 95% CI and p-values reported for all screen modalities

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric results: prospective analysis β=-0.07 for total screen time (95% CI -0.09, -0.05, p<0.01); cross-sectional β=-0.07 (95% CI -0.09, -0.05, p<0.01); dose-response associations reported for categorical screen time

FT_EC7_reason: Design is both prospective and cross-sectional, not purely longitudinal-DV-only. Screen time is analyzed as IV predicting nutrition outcomes
notes: Strong v6 fit. All criteria met. Sample includes diverse demographics (49% female, 56.9% white, 43.1% non-white). Propensity weights applied. Both baseline and 1-year follow-up data analyzed

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 3 (2019-2021), ages 12-13 years, n=9859, 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis; multiple linear regression estimating associations between transgender/gender-questioning identity and screen use modalities plus problematic use measures

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Annals of Epidemiology, 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen use operationalized across modalities: recreational screen time (TV/movies, single/multi-player gaming, texting, social media, video chat, internet browsing) and problematic use (SMAQ for social media, VGAQ for video games, MPIQ for mobile phones). Outcome is transgender/gender-questioning identity, an individual-trait psychological/identity-based construct within well-being domain. All SM and phone variables isolate specific modalities

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative inferential analysis using multiple linear regression comparing screen use by gender identity status

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients with 95% CI reported for all outcomes: transgender vs cisgender β=4.51 h/d total screen time (95% CI 1.17-7.85, p=0.008); problematic social media β=0.43 (95% CI 0.17-0.69, p=0.001); problematic video games β=0.46 (95% CI 0.19-0.74, p=0.001)

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal designs
notes: Strong v6 fit. All criteria met. Sample diverse: 48.8% female, 47.6% racial/ethnic minority, 1.0% transgender, 1.1% gender-questioning. Propensity weights applied. Multiple modalities tested separately

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Years 2-3 (2018-2021), ages 11-12 at baseline, n=9398, 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis; regression models (logistic, ordinal logistic, multiple linear) examining bedtime screen behaviors (Year 2) and sleep outcomes (Year 3)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Journal of Adolescent Health, 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen use operationalized as bedtime-specific behaviors: TV/movies, video games, music, phone (talking/texting), social media, chat rooms, browsing, computer/laptop, reading, plus device presence (TV/Internet in bedroom) and phone management strategies. All isolate phone/SM/video modalities separately. Outcome variables are sleep duration (self-reported) and sleep disturbance (caregiver-reported Sleep Disturbance Scale for Children), individual-trait health/behavioral constructs. No demographic-only outcomes

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative inferential analysis using logistic, ordinal logistic, and linear regression models comparing bedtime screen use to sleep outcomes

FT_IC4.evidence: Extensive numeric results: TV/device in bedroom β=-0.21 h/night sleep duration (95% CI -0.28, -0.13, p<0.001); leaving ringer on OR=1.25 for sleep disturbance (95% CI 1.00-1.56, p=0.046); texting β=-0.17 h/night (95% CI -0.22, -0.12, p<0.001); social media β=-0.21 h/night (95% CI -0.26, -0.16, p<0.001)

FT_EC7_reason: Prospective design, but screen use serves as IV (bedtime behaviors predict sleep outcomes); FT-EC7 requires DV-only with no IV analyses. Screen exposure includes phone use as predictor of sleep outcome
notes: Strong v6 fit. All criteria met. Sample diverse: 48.4% female, 45% racial/ethnic minority. Propensity weights applied. Multiple bedtime screen behaviors tested separately. 1-year prospective follow-up with adjustment for baseline sleep variables and multiple confounders
